title: AI tool reveals climate shifts may have fueled bursts of bird evolution
excerpt: University of Michigan researchers have used an AI tool to demonstrate that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid evolutionary bursts and that these bursts frequently coincided with climate shifts throughout Earth's history.
content_html:
University of Michigan researchers have used an AI tool to demonstrate that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid evolutionary bursts and that these bursts frequently coincided with climate shifts throughout Earth's history.

Evolutionary theory predicts that the evolution of organisms occurs in pulsed bursts followed by slowdowns—something researchers have seen hints of in the fossil record.

Now, looking at passerines, University of Michigan researchers have identified these evolutionary bursts by examining data gleaned from skeletal measurements of contemporary bird specimens. The researchers also observed that these bursts coincided with historical climate change.

"This is really important for evolutionary theory because there's a long history, going back 100 years, that predicts the emergence of new groups, called evolutionary radiations, is often associated with an explosive burst of diversification. Evolutionary theory predicts that adaptive radiations may account for a large portion of the diversity of life on Earth," said Jake Berv, lead author of the study and postdoctoral fellow in the U-M School for Environment and Sustainability.

"This could be because of a new ecological opportunity, or it could be because a group dispersed to a new continent, resulting in dramatic accelerations in their rate of evolution. The idea is that, over time, there's less opportunity as evolution proceeds, and so it slows down, and that this occurs in pulses across time. That's what theory predicts, and that seems to be what we see in the data as well."

The findings, deduced using artificial intelligence and a large-scale statistical model, are published in Nature Ecology & Evolution.

Seeing with Skelevision

To trace how this group of birds evolved, the U-M scientists, including senior author Brian Weeks, examined more than 2,000 species and collected more than 170,000 individual skeletal measurements. To create such a large dataset, the team used an AI tool called Skelevision, which Weeks' lab developed with David Fouhey's lab at New York University.

Skelevision uses a camera to photograph specimens—for this study, bird skeletons—against a background grid that provides a common scale. Over a seven-year collaboration, Weeks and Fouhey developed an AI model that can precisely measure a dozen bones across the avian skeleton.

The researchers used Skelevision to scan and measure more than 15,000 individual museum specimens, most of which came from the U-M Museum of Zoology collections. Scanning each specimen takes only about 45 seconds, which makes it possible to digitize entire collections.

A method to the madness

Berv then developed a new statistical method called bifrost that enabled researchers to examine a species' entire skeleton at once. This allowed the researchers to estimate the evolution of body shape over the approximately 45-million-year history of Passeriformes.

"The whole organism is an integrated, complex morphology, and each of the individual pieces is interrelated to every other part in the body," Berv said. "The question from the model's perspective is, 'What is the sequence of evolutionary changes that needs to happen to explain the variation we can see today?'"

The researchers found that passerines underwent rapid bursts of body-shape evolution roughly 35 million years ago, coinciding with the Eocene–Oligocene transition. During this time, Earth experienced intense global cooling. Then, the statistical analysis showed a cluster of evolutionary slowdowns that occurred about 15 million years ago and coincided with another important geological event.

"Our findings have definitely shifted my thinking about how the world works," said Weeks, associate professor of ecosystem science and management at U-M's School for Environment and Sustainability.

"This pattern we found with rare, big increases in the rates of evolution and lots of small decreases in the rates of evolution is really consistent with a pattern where lineages are exploring new ecological space and changing rapidly to take advantage of that opportunity."

A bonus discovery

To test their results, the researchers then examined the skeleton dataset worldwide. They found that where birds live also predicts their average rate of morphological evolution—that is, communities at more extreme latitudes and with greater seasonal temperature fluctuations tend to host species that evolve more quickly than those near the equator.

The similar patterns the researchers found across time and space suggest that the shared influence of environmental variation may be an important cause of changes in body shape.

"It looks like there's a connection between latitudinal gradients and rates of morphological evolution that has been underappreciated," Weeks said. "I hope our findings will inspire a new integration of rates of morphological change into other big areas of interest, things like the very well-known latitudinal gradients in biodiversity."

The study itself points to the importance of investing in museum collections, Weeks says, and how advances in artificial intelligence can help researchers better leverage these resources.

"It's especially clear how important it is to invest in museums when you think about the scale of an analysis like this; it's so far beyond the scope of what can be done using specimens contributed by an individual collector," he said.

"It's also fun to imagine what early collectors would make of how we're using the specimens they collected—I imagine it would blow their minds to learn that a computer has analyzed a photograph of these specimens. It's just another example of how impossible it is to foresee the full future value of a specimen."

The researchers say the study may also offer lessons for how species respond to our current era of intense climate change.

"Right now, we're in this moment in human history where there's dramatic global climate change, and we don't know what's going to happen over even a 10-year period, let alone over a 10-million-year period," Berv said.

"To have a chance of understanding the long-term impact of human activity on Earth, we have to study the relationship between events in Earth's history and evolutionary transitions."

Publication details

Rates of passerine body plan evolution in time and space, Nature Ecology & Evolution (2026). DOI: 10.1038/s41559-026-03110-5

Who's behind this story?

Sadie Harley

BSc Life Sciences & Ecology. Microbiology lab background with pharmaceutical news experience in oil, gas, and renewable industries. Full profile →

Andrew Zinin

Master's in physics with research experience. Long-time science news enthusiast. Plays key role in Science X's editorial success. Full profile →

sentences.en: University of Michigan researchers found that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid bursts that coincided with climatic shifts.
sentences.zh: 密歇根大学的研究人员发现，雀形目鸟类的进化与气候变化相一致。

blocks.html_en: University of Michigan researchers have used an AI tool to demonstrate that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid evolutionary bursts and that these bursts frequently coincided with climate shifts throughout Earth's history.

sentences.en: University of Michigan researchers have used an AI tool to demonstrate that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid evolutionary bursts and that these bursts frequently coincided with climate shifts throughout Earth's history.
sentences.zh: 密歇根大学的研究人员使用人工智能工具证明，雀形目鸟类是在快速进化爆发中进化的，并且这些爆发经常与整个地球历史上的气候变化同时发生。

blocks.html_en: Evolutionary theory predicts that the evolution of organisms occurs in pulsed bursts followed by slowdowns—something researchers have seen hints of in the fossil record.

blocks.html_en: Now, looking at passerines, University of Michigan researchers have identified these evolutionary bursts by examining data gleaned from skeletal measurements of contemporary bird specimens. The researchers also observed that these bursts coincided with historical climate change.

sentences.en: The researchers also observed that these bursts coincided with historical climate change.
sentences.zh: 研究人员还观察到，这些爆发与历史上的气候变化同时发生。

sentences.zh: “这对于进化论来说非常重要，因为有一个可以追溯到 100 年前的悠久历史，它预测新群体的出现，称为进化辐射，通常与多样化的爆炸性爆发相关。

sentences.en: Evolutionary theory predicts that adaptive radiations may account for a large portion of the diversity of life on Earth," said Jake Berv, lead author of the study and postdoctoral fellow in the U-M School for Environment and Sustainability.
sentences.zh: 进化理论预测，适应性辐射可能是地球上生命多样性的很大一部分原因。”该研究的主要作者、密西根大学环境与可持续发展学院的博士后杰克·贝尔夫（Jake Berv）说。

sentences.zh: “这可能是因为一个新的生态机会，也可能是因为一个群体分散到了新大陆，导致其进化速度急剧加快。

sentences.en: The idea is that, over time, there's less opportunity as evolution proceeds, and so it slows down, and that this occurs in pulses across time.

sentences.en: That's what theory predicts, and that seems to be what we see in the data as well."
sentences.zh: 这就是理论的预测，这似乎也是我们在数据中看到的。”

blocks.html_en: The researchers used Skelevision to scan and measure more than 15,000 individual museum specimens, most of which came from the U-M Museum of Zoology collections. Scanning each specimen takes only about 45 seconds, which makes it possible to digitize entire collections.

blocks.html_en: "It's especially clear how important it is to invest in museums when you think about the scale of an analysis like this; it's so far beyond the scope of what can be done using specimens contributed by an individual collector," he said.

sentences.en: "It's especially clear how important it is to invest in museums when you think about the scale of an analysis like this; it's so far beyond the scope of what can be done using specimens contributed by an individual collector," he said.
sentences.zh: 他说：“当你考虑到这样的分析规模时，就特别清楚投资博物馆是多么重要；它远远超出了使用个人收藏家贡献的标本所能完成的范围。”

blocks.html_en: "It's also fun to imagine what early collectors would make of how we're using the specimens they collected—I imagine it would blow their minds to learn that a computer has analyzed a photograph of these specimens. It's just another example of how impossible it is to foresee the full future value of a specimen."

sentences.en: "It's also fun to imagine what early collectors would make of how we're using the specimens they collected—I imagine it would blow their minds to learn that a computer has analyzed a photograph of these specimens.
sentences.zh: “想象早期的收藏家会如何看待我们如何使用他们收集的标本也很有趣——我想如果他们知道计算机已经分析了这些标本的照片，他们一定会大吃一惊。

sentences.en: It's just another example of how impossible it is to foresee the full future value of a specimen."
sentences.zh: 这只是另一个例子，说明预测一个标本的全部未来价值是多么不可能。”

blocks.html_en: The researchers say the study may also offer lessons for how species respond to our current era of intense climate change.

sentences.en: The researchers say the study may also offer lessons for how species respond to our current era of intense climate change.
sentences.zh: 研究人员表示，这项研究还可能为物种如何应对当今剧烈气候变化的时代提供经验教训。

blocks.html_en: "Right now, we're in this moment in human history where there's dramatic global climate change, and we don't know what's going to happen over even a 10-year period, let alone over a 10-million-year period," Berv said.

sentences.en: "Right now, we're in this moment in human history where there's dramatic global climate change, and we don't know what's going to happen over even a 10-year period, let alone over a 10-million-year period," Berv said.
sentences.zh: 贝尔夫说：“目前，我们正处于人类历史上的这个时刻，全球气候正在发生剧烈变化，我们甚至不知道十年后会发生什么，更不用说一千万年了。”

blocks.html_en: "To have a chance of understanding the long-term impact of human activity on Earth, we have to study the relationship between events in Earth's history and evolutionary transitions."

sentences.en: "To have a chance of understanding the long-term impact of human activity on Earth, we have to study the relationship between events in Earth's history and evolutionary transitions."
sentences.zh: “为了有机会了解人类活动对地球的长期影响，我们必须研究地球历史事件与进化转变之间的关系。”

content_html_zh:
密歇根大学的研究人员使用人工智能工具证明，雀形目鸟类是在快速进化爆发中进化的，并且这些爆发经常与整个地球历史上的气候变化同时发生。

进化论预测，生物体的进化发生在脉冲爆发和随后的减速中——研究人员已经在化石记录中看到了这一点。

现在，密歇根大学的研究人员通过检查从当代鸟类标本的骨骼测量中收集的数据，发现了雀形目鸟类的进化爆发。 研究人员还观察到，这些爆发与历史上的气候变化同时发生。

“这对于进化论来说非常重要，因为有一个可以追溯到 100 年前的悠久历史，它预测新群体的出现，称为进化辐射，通常与多样化的爆炸性爆发相关。 进化理论预测，适应性辐射可能是地球上生命多样性的很大一部分原因。”该研究的主要作者、密西根大学环境与可持续发展学院的博士后杰克·贝尔夫（Jake Berv）说。

“这可能是因为一个新的生态机会，也可能是因为一个群体分散到了新大陆，导致其进化速度急剧加快。 这个想法是，随着时间的推移，随着进化的进行，机会越来越少，因此它会减慢速度，并且这种情况会随着时间的推移以脉冲的形式发生。 这就是理论的预测，这似乎也是我们在数据中看到的。”

该研究结果是利用人工智能和大规模统计模型得出的，发表在《自然生态学与进化》上。

使用 Skelevision 观看

为了追踪这群鸟类的进化过程，包括资深作者 Brian Weeks 在内的密歇根大学科学家检查了 2,000 多个物种，并收集了超过 170,000 个个体骨骼测量数据。 为了创建如此大的数据集，该团队使用了一种名为 Skelevision 的人工智能工具，该工具是 Weeks 的实验室与纽约大学 David Fouhey 的实验室共同开发的。

Skelevision 使用相机在提供通用比例的背景网格上拍摄标本（在本研究中为鸟类骨骼）。 经过七年的合作，Weeks 和 Fouhey 开发了一种人工智能模型，可以精确测量鸟类骨骼中的十几块骨头。

研究人员使用 Skelevision 扫描并测量了 15,000 多个博物馆标本，其中大部分来自密歇根大学动物学博物馆的藏品。 扫描每个标本仅需约 45 秒，这使得将整个藏品数字化成为可能。

一种疯狂的方法

贝尔夫随后开发了一种称为 bifrost 的新统计方法，使研究人员能够立即检查一个物种的整个骨骼。 这使得研究人员能够估计雀形目大约 4500 万年历史中体形的演变。

贝尔夫说：“整个有机体是一个完整的、复杂的形态，每个单独的部分都与身体的其他部分相互关联。” “从模型的角度来看，问题是，‘需要发生什么样的进化变化序列才能解释我们今天看到的变异？’”

研究人员发现，雀形目鸟类在大约 3500 万年前经历了体形的快速进化，恰逢始新世-渐新世过渡期。 在此期间，地球经历了剧烈的全球变冷。 然后，统计分析显示，大约 1500 万年前发生了一系列进化放缓，并且与另一个重要的地质事件同时发生。

密西根大学环境与可持续发展学院生态系统科学与管理副教授威克斯说：“我们的发现无疑改变了我对世界如何运转的看法。”

“我们发现这种模式，进化率出现罕见的大幅增长，而进化率出现大量小幅下降，这与谱系正在探索新的生态空间并迅速变化以利用这一机会的模式确实一致。”

额外的发现

为了测试他们的结果，研究人员随后检查了全世界的骨骼数据集。 他们发现，鸟类居住的地方也可以预测它们形态进化的平均速度，也就是说，处于更极端纬度和季节性温度波动更大的群落往往比赤道附近的物种进化得更快。

研究人员在时间和空间上发现的类似模式表明，环境变化的共同影响可能是体形变化的重要原因。

“看来纬度梯度和形态进化速率之间存在联系，但这一联系一直被低估，”威克斯说。 “我希望我们的发现能够激发人们将形态变化率重新整合到其他重要领域，例如众所周知的生物多样性纬度梯度。”

威克斯说，这项研究本身指出了投资博物馆藏品的重要性，以及人工智能的进步如何帮助研究人员更好地利用这些资源。

他说：“当你考虑到这样的分析规模时，就特别清楚投资博物馆是多么重要；它远远超出了使用个人收藏家贡献的标本所能完成的范围。”

“想象早期的收藏家会如何看待我们如何使用他们收集的标本也很有趣——我想如果他们知道计算机已经分析了这些标本的照片，他们一定会大吃一惊。 这只是另一个例子，说明预测一个标本的全部未来价值是多么不可能。”

研究人员表示，这项研究还可能为物种如何应对当今剧烈气候变化的时代提供经验教训。

贝尔夫说：“目前，我们正处于人类历史上的这个时刻，全球气候正在发生剧烈变化，我们甚至不知道十年后会发生什么，更不用说一千万年了。”

“为了有机会了解人类活动对地球的长期影响，我们必须研究地球历史事件与进化转变之间的关系。”

出版详情

雀形目动物身体计划在时间和空间上的进化速率，自然生态与进化（2026）。 DOI：10.1038/s41559-026-03110-5
